channel.title: NYT > Science
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: A Fiery Visitor From Space Photo-Bombs an Erupting Volcano
item.description: For a split second, a fireball outshone the rivers of molten lava flowing from the Philippines’ most active volcano.
item.dc:creator: Robin George Andrews

item.title: NASA’s Moon Base Plan Adds Two Rovers for Its Astronauts
item.description: The contracts, announced on Tuesday, are part of the space agency’s efforts to establish a moon base.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang

item.title: U.S. Aims to Give Cold War Plutonium to Start-Ups for Nuclear Fuel
item.description: Companies say it’s a better way to dispose of the Cold War-era material — and fix a shortage of nuclear fuel. But the plan has also faced criticism from nonproliferation experts.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer
item.category: Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
item.media:description: Work with radioactive materials at a plutonium facility at the at Los Alamos National Laboratory in 1978.

item.title: Where to See the Total Solar Eclipse on Aug. 12
item.description: On Aug. 12, parts of Greenland, Iceland, Spain and Portugal will experience the thrill of daytime darkness. Here’s where you can witness the cosmic spectacle.
item.dc:creator: Danielle Dowling
item.media:credit: Nanna Heitmann for The New York Times
item.media:description: Total darkness will last a little over two minutes in parts of the Westfjords region of Iceland.

item.title: Scientists Ditched a Scary Climate Scenario. What Now?
item.description: While global warming is still a threat, the decision to back away from a worst-case outlook raises questions about whether some risks have been overstated.
item.dc:creator: Brad Plumer and Eric Niiler
item.media:description: A wildfire burning in the Simi Valley of California this month.

item.title: A Very Lonely Caterpillar, Possibly the Last of Its Kind, Has Died
item.description: The Sacramento Mountains checkerspot butterfly hasn’t been seen in the wild since 2022. The caterpillar was the last individual in human care.
item.dc:creator: Catrin Einhorn
item.media:credit: Diana Cervantes for The New York Times

item.title: How to Stay Safe Around Jellyfish This Beach Season
item.description: Jellyfish myths and misconceptions abound. Here’s how to stay safe this beach season around these gelatinous wonders.
item.dc:creator: Johnny Diaz
item.media:description: A jellyfish at the Steinhart Aquarium at the California Academy of Sciences in San Francisco. Jellyfish play important roles in the marine ecosystem and are a key source of food for some fish and sea turtles.

item.title: SpaceX Completes Mostly Successful Starship Rocket Flight
item.description: The 12th test flight of SpaceX’s gargantuan rocket launched on Friday evening and ended its journey in the Indian Ocean just over an hour later.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:description: Sunrise at Starbase in South Texas on Friday, as the SpaceX Starship rocket was preparing for another launch attempt.

item.title: A New Era of Exploring the Universe in Radio
item.description: With 263 antennas spread across the U.S. and Mexico, the Next Generation Very Large Array, would join a new wave of radio astronomy.
item.dc:creator: Katrina Miller

item.title: Eli Lilly to Buy 3 Vaccine Developers
item.description: The drugmaker said it would spend up to $4 billion to acquire small companies exploring vaccines for shingles, Epstein-Barr virus and other pathogens.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Robbins
item.media:description: Eli Lilly has a long history in infectious diseases.

item.title: A Powerful H.I.V. Drug Lands in Zambia. But Will It Reach Those Who Need It?
item.description: Lenacapavir, which protects people from H.I.V. infection with twice-yearly shots, is arriving in a country where the health system has been hollowed out by American aid cuts.
item.dc:creator: Stephanie Nolen and Arlette Bashizi
item.category: Global Fund to Fight AIDS, Tuberculosis and Malaria
President's Emergency Plan for AIDS Relief
item.media:description: Health workers promoted a new H.I.V. prevention method at the University of Zambia in Lusaka in March and encouraged students to sign up to get it.

item.title: Threat of Explosion From Toxic Chemical Tank Lessened, Officials Say
item.description: The risk of a large explosion has been averted, officials say. But a smaller blast remains possible, and 16,000 people remain displaced.
item.dc:creator: Reis Thebault, Heather Knight, Shawn Hubler and Christina Morales
item.media:description: An aerial view of water being sprayed onto a tank at GKN Aerospace in Garden Grove, Calif., on Saturday.

item.title: One-and-Done Heart Disease Prevention? Scientists Show It May Be Possible.
item.description: A single infusion of an experimental gene-editing drug seemed to reduce LDL long-term in a small trial. The results may point to something “curative,” one expert said.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata
item.media:description: A colored transmission electron micrograph of low-density lipoprotein, or LDL, particles.

item.title: Rafe Pomerance, the Paul Revere of Climate Change, Dies at 79
item.description: An environmental lobbyist and activist, he was a pivotal figure in drawing public attention and political support to the existential issue.
item.dc:creator: Trip Gabriel
item.media:description: Rafe Pomerance as president of the environmental group Friends of the Earth, in 1983. “He recruited us and energized us and gave us facts that allowed us to work on the most important issue of our time,” said Daniel Becker, a longtime leader with the Sierra Club.

item.title: RFK Jr.’s Push to Curb Antidepressants Has Shaken Psychiatry
item.description: An annual psychiatric meeting was abuzz over Robert F. Kennedy Jr.’s call to rein in the use of depression medications. Some fear it will drive patients away from care.
item.dc:creator: Ellen Barry
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times

item.title: 5 Takeaways From a Times Investigation on Autism Therapy Clinics
item.description: A rapidly growing industry often overprescribes treatment to young children with autism, who spend as many as 40 hours a week at the facilities.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kliff, Margot Sanger-Katz and Asmaa Elkeurti
item.media:description: A clinic for autistic children in Concord, N.C., that uses applied behavioral analysis, one of the largest and fastest-growing kinds of medical care for state Medicaid programs.

item.title: Short Naps, Long Hours: How Autism Clinics Squeeze Medicaid Dollars Out of Preschoolers
item.description: The industry has grown rapidly, straining state budgets. A focus on finances has led to overbilling, fraud and even harm.
item.dc:creator: Sarah Kliff, Margot Sanger-Katz, Erin Schaff and Asmaa Elkeurti

item.title: When Humans Went Away, the Wildlife Strayed
item.description: When people disappeared from the landscape, as they did during the pandemic, wild animals changed how they used space and resources, scientists found.
item.dc:creator: Emily Anthes
item.media:credit: Loren Elliott for The New York Times
item.media:description: A mule deer captured by a trail camera as it walked along a game trail in the Verdugo Mountains in Glendale, Calif.

item.title: Trump Eases Restrictions on Climate ‘Super Pollutants’
item.description: The administration is delaying a phaseout of hydrofluorocarbons, potent planet-warming chemicals used in air-conditioning and refrigeration.
item.dc:creator: Lisa Friedman and Maxine Joselow
item.media:description: Replacing an air conditioning unit outside a home in western Pennsylvania during a heat wave last June.

item.title: Experimental Drug Yields Dramatic Weight Loss
item.description: People who got the injection, retatrutide, lost 28 percent of their body weight on average after 80 weeks, Eli Lilly said.
item.dc:creator: Gina Kolata and Rebecca Robbins
item.media:description: Eli Lilly’s headquarters in Indianapolis.

item.title: SpaceX Scrubs Launch Attempt of Updated Starship Rocket
item.description: The giant rocket was scheduled to lift off on Thursday after a seven-month pause. SpaceX could try again as soon as Friday.
item.dc:creator: Kenneth Chang
item.media:description: The booster for the SpaceX rocket Starship being rolled out to the launchpad at the Starbase in South Texas.

item.title: Science Group Seeks Public Hearing for N.S.F. Nominee
item.description: The American Association for the Advancement of Science questioned the credentials of Jim O’Neill, tapped to lead the National Science Foundation.
item.dc:creator: William J. Broad
item.category: American Assn for the Advancement of Science
Senate Committee on Health, Education, Labor and Pensions
item.media:credit: Amy Rossetti/Department of Health and Human Services, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Jim O’Neill, left, being sworn in last June as deputy to the health secretary, Robert F. Kennedy Jr.

item.title: A Powerful El Niño Is Forming. If History Is a Guide, It Could Hit Hard.
item.description: The biggest episodes of the past have altered the course of human events, according to researchers. An emerging one is drawing historic comparisons.
item.dc:creator: Chico Harlan
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:description: An illustration published in 1877 depicted a scene in southern India. Millions died from a famine that coincided with an El Niño that year.

item.title: Pigeons and People Have Been Frenemies for Longer Than You Think
item.description: Bones discovered at an archaeological site in Cyprus suggest the birds have been strutting around human settlements since at least 1400 B.C.
item.dc:creator: Rachel Nuwer
item.media:credit: Angelina Katsanis for The New York Times
item.media:description: A new study leads scientists to wonder whether people set out to domesticate pigeons or the process occurred naturally as pigeons “colonized” human environments.

item.title: Musk’s SpaceX Reveals Its Finances for the First Time as It Readies for IPO
item.description: Mr. Musk’s rocket and satellite maker disclosed its financial performance as it prepares to go public in what is set to be one of the largest offerings to date.
item.dc:creator: Ryan Mac and Lauren Hirsch
item.media:credit: Meridith Kohut for The New York Times
item.media:description: A successful SpaceX offering would bring riches to Wall Street, the company’s employees and, of course, Elon Musk, who is already the world’s richest person.

item.title: This Moon of Neptune Might Have Survived a Wrecking-Ball Event
item.description: Neptune’s moons have long provided evidence of a cataclysm four billion years ago. A new study suggests one moon may have made it through.
item.dc:creator: Jonathan O’Callaghan

item.title: Ebola Crisis Sparks Debate Over Global Health Double Standards
item.description: To some Africans, the claim that the continent’s largest health agency had already bungled its response scratched a familiar wound.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.category: Africa Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:description: Red Cross workers carry the coffin of a person who died of Ebola at a health center in Rwampara, the Democratic Republic of Congo, on Wednesday.

item.title: Europe’s Green-Energy Future Has a Reindeer Problem
item.description: In northern Norway, Sami people fear a copper mine will disrupt their traditional lifestyles.
item.dc:creator: Alexa Robles-Gil, Sachi Kitajima Mulkey and Michal Siarek
item.media:credit: Michał Siarek for The New York Times
